obligatory thumbnail imageMany articles online cover deep and complex topics about web development. But in order to gain understanding of them, sometimes we need to go back to the basics. The original three pillars of the web. HTML, CSS and JS. I’ll throw a short overview of the most popular libraries and frameworks as a bonus.
HTML
HTML is a markup programming language originally created at CERN. Once they’ve got computers with browsers in there, scientists quickly figured out how useful and beneficial a digital system of documents can be. They could create basic document layouts, bold words or sentences, make links to other documents, etc. Why bother going to the physical archives and dig through a ton of papers to quickly check the mass of a photon, when you can simply fire up google and type a few words to get the same result within seconds?
Html has many tags, but the most important one is div. There are a few more which are required for the page to work, but the rest of them are mostly useless and add unnecessary complications and learning overhead. Keep it simple, silly.
CSS
CSS (not to be confused with Control Stick Steering while operating a space shuttle) brings colors into your documents. When internet became available to the public thanks to Bill Gates, sharing documents (now called web-sites) was very useful to the average person too. However, aesthetics of scientific papers are almost nonexistent. That’s why CSS was added. Content Style Source, or CSS, is as the name implies a source of styling rules for a web page. Like html, CSS has one useful property and plenty of un-useful ones. I’m talking about CSS classes of course. This is a CSS class for example:
.my-class {/*style rules here*/}You can write more complex commands such as:
#gallery > form.create > input['file'] {}but these are mostly arcane, useless and hard to read. I’m not even sure if they’re called commands. Stick with CSS classes and you should be golden. Combined with only using divs and BEM methodology, it creates the best possible solution for maintaining the appearance of your content.
JS
JS, short of JavaScript is a scripting language based on Java. It was introduced to add interactivity to the page. It can manipulate both HTML and CSS. It can even manipulate itself. Since JS can run in any browser, it is considered a cross-platform language as well. These days, however, a superset and a proper programming language was introduced. TypeScript. It adds type-safety to the JS, but it doesn’t run in the browser. It has to be compiled to the pure JS first.

As applications grew in complexity, libraries and frameworks emerged to simplify the workflow.
React
React is a simple JS library. It has a whole ecosystem of utility packages designed specifically for it. These days there’s even a compiler. Although whenever you create a React project things end up completely revolving around it, React is still fairly unobtrusive and lightweight.
It is hailed for bringing several innovative solutions to already solved problems, and is now the absolute king in the industry of the web development.
Angular
Angular is considered to be a “batteries included” framework for the web. Many people don’t like it, because it comes with stuff you normally don’t need in your average web app. Routing or state management solutions are rarely used, and should be added only when needed. It also has a steeper learning curve because it requires knowledge of object oriented, reactive declarative programming concepts, dependency injection and other things average software engineers are usually unaware of. Typescript is also mandatory here.
Svelte, Vue, Astro
These three frameworks are popular, but only on Reddit. They should be avoided because they’re not backed by a large company such as Google or Meta. In todays world, only big corporations can deliver stable, good and long term supported products. This is very self evident, especially when you look at gaming or web related software.
Conclusion
’Tis a short one, but I still hope that this article was informative and insightful.
Thank You for reading 🙋‍♂️
